section.title: Introduction
section.p: The CMHG file format (also used by CMunge) is a domain-specific language used to define the interfaces and structure of a RISC OS relocatable module. CMunge processes these description files to generate the assembly language "veneers" required to bridge the RISC OS kernel environment with C or C++ application code.

section.title: Overview
section.p: A CMHG file is a sequence of logical lines containing directives. Each directive consists of a keyword, a colon, and one or more parameters. CMHG and CMunge handle the low-level complexities of the RISC OS module header, ensuring that the C environment (stack, registers, etc.) is correctly initialized before calling user-written C functions.
CMunge extends the original CMHG format with additional directives for error management, specialized veneers, and support for modern architectures (32-bit and 64-bit).

subsection.title: Syntax Rules
subsection.p:
The CMHG/CMunge parser follows these rules:
Logical Lines: The file consists of a sequence of logical lines.
Comments: Start with a semicolon (;) and extend to the end of the physical line. All comments are removed before parsing.
Directives: Keywords followed by a colon (:). Keywords are case-insensitive and consist of alphabetic characters and minus signs.
Line Continuation: A physical line ending with a comma (,) indicates the logical line continues onto the next physical line.
Strings: Enclosed in double quotes ("). Adjacent quoted strings (even if separated by whitespace or newlines) are concatenated before processing.
Numbers: Unsigned decimal by default. Hexadecimal if prefixed with & (RISC OS style) or 0x (C style).
Arguments: Lists of arguments may be separated by commas or spaces. Commas are required when splitting lists across lines.

subsection.title: Integer Evaluation
subsection.p: CMHG strips any surrounding parentheses from integer values to allow values to be used from C-style header files through #include directives.
CMunge also supports simple additive operations on integer values. Expressions such as (&800 + 1) are acceptable. No other arithmetic operations are supported.

category.title: title-string
category.p: Defines the module's internal name (as seen by *Modules). It should ideally be less than 16 characters and must be a single word.

category.title: help-string
category.p: Defines the descriptive string displayed by *Help Modules. The format is typically:
The version number should be in the form d.dd.

category.title: date-string
category.p: (CMunge) An optional string specifying the build date. This is used if the developer feels the date is a different reference to the version number.

category.title: initialisation-code
category.p: Specifies the C function called when the module is initialized or reinitialized.
C Prototype: _kernel_oserror *init(const char *tail, int podule_base, void *pw);

item.p: tail: Points to the string of arguments the module was invoked with.
podule_base: The base address of the podule from which the module was invoked (or 0).
pw: The module's private word. This should generally not be used except when required for OS calls (e.g., setting up veneers via OS_Claim).

category.p: Return NULL for success, or a pointer to an error block otherwise. Finalisation code will not be called if initialization fails.

category.title: finalisation-code
category.p: Specifies the C function called when the module is being killed (just before the C library is killed).
C Prototype: _kernel_oserror *final(int fatal, int podule, void *pw);
fatal contains the value of R10 on entry. Return NULL to allow termination, or an error pointer to refuse. Causing an abort in this routine will leave a 'dead' module in the chain.

category.title: service-call-handler
category.p: Specifies the C function for handling service calls. Optionally followed by a list of service numbers to filter for. The generated veneer passes only matching service calls to the handler; if no numbers are given, all service calls are passed, which is not recommended.
C Prototype: void *service(int number, _kernel_swi_regs *r, void *pw);

When handling service calls it may be useful to have a pointer to the base of the module in memory. Use the -zbase command-line option to have CMunge define extern int Image__RO_Base in the generated header.

category.title: module-is-runnable
category.p: If specified, the module can be run as an application (e.g., via *Run). The C main function will be called in user mode.
C Prototype: int main(int argc, char *argv[]);

category.title: module-is-not-reentrant
category.p: Declares that the module is not reentrant. This prevents the module being entered again if it is already running.

category.title: module-is-c-plus-plus
category.p: (CMunge) Indicates the module includes C++ code built using CFront. CMunge generates code to call global constructors via ______main during initialization.

category.title: module-is-initialised-early
category.p: (CMunge) Declares that the module should be initialized early in the boot sequence, primarily for ROM-resident modules.

category.title: swi-chunk-base-number
category.p: The base SWI number for the module's chunk (64 SWIs). Bit 17 (X-bit) must not be set.

category.title: swi-handler-code
category.p: The central dispatcher for all SWIs in the chunk.
C Prototype: _kernel_oserror *swi_handler(int number, _kernel_swi_regs *r, void *pw);
number is the offset (0-63). Registers R0-R9 on input can be updated via r->r[n] to change return values.

category.title: swi-decoding-table
category.p: Defines the SWI prefix and the names for the decoding table.
The first entry is the SWI prefix. Each subsequent name entry may be followed by /<handler> to use a particular routine to handle that SWI instead of the central handler.

category.title: swi-decoding-code
category.p: Provides custom name-to-number and number-to-name translations. Supports 1-function (legacy) and 2-function interfaces.

If r[0] < 0, a name-to-number conversion is requested: r[1] points to a control-terminated (not null-terminated) string. If recognised, set r[0] to the SWI offset within the block; otherwise leave r[0] negative.

Otherwise, a number-to-name conversion is requested: r[0] is the SWI offset, r[1] points to the output buffer, r[2] is the current write offset into that buffer, and r[3] points to the byte beyond the end of the buffer. Write the name (without a terminator) starting at r[2] and update r[2] to reflect the number of bytes written.

p.code: int name2number(const char *string, void *pw); int number2name(int number, char *buffer, int offset, int end, void *pw);

category.p: name2number converts a control-terminated string to a SWI offset (0–63); return a negative value if unrecognised. number2name writes the unterminated name into buffer starting at offset and returns the updated offset; if there is no name for the given offset, return offset unchanged.

category.title: command-keyword-table
category.p: Defines the module's * command table. The first parameter is the name of the central command handler function. It may be specified as - if every command in the table has its own handler: sub-keyword or no-handler: flag, in which case no central handler is needed.
C Prototype for default handler: _kernel_oserror *command(const char *arg_string, int argc, int number, void *pw);
number is the index of the command that was invoked, counting from 0 in the order they appear in the table.

Each command is described using a set of sub-keywords in parentheses:
Minimum arguments (0-255, default 0). Maximum arguments (0-255, default 0). Bitmap indicating which arguments to GSTrans (default 0). Marks it as a filing system command. Syntax/help messages use tokens for translation. Appends the syntax string to the help text. Command is for *Configure / *Status. Equivalent to configure. Handler is called for help message processing (unsupported in some versions). Syntax error string/token (default empty). Help string/token (default empty). Specific C function to call for this command. Command has no code handler (help-only).
Special handler behaviour for different invocation types:

definition-table.definition: arg_string points to a buffer to write into. Cast it using the help_PRINT_BUFFER macro, fill it with a zero-terminated string, and return the initial value of arg_string. Return NULL to print nothing.
arg_string is arg_CONFIGURE_SYNTAX (a cast of 0). Print the syntax and return NULL.
arg_string is arg_STATUS (a cast of 1). Print the current status and return NULL.

Use arg_string and argc to apply the configuration. Return NULL on success, an error pointer on failure, or one of the special values defined in the generated header:
The option was not recognised. A numeric argument was required but not given. The value given was too large. Too many parameters were supplied.

subsection.title: Veneers and Callbacks
subsection.p: Veneers create entry points that bridge RISC OS vectors/events to C functions. They are defined as EntryName[/HandlerName]([params]).

category.title: generic-veneers
category.p: Used for general callbacks (e.g., from OS_AddCallBack). The optional parameters are:

definition-table.definition: The register in which the private word is passed on entry to the veneer. Defaults to R12. Normally R12 is not preserved by a generic veneer unless a private-word register is specified. To force R12 to be preserved without using it as the private word source, specify private-word: r12 explicitly.
The handler may return VENEER_SETCARRY instead of an error pointer or NULL. This causes the carry flag to be set on return. All registers are taken from the register block as normal.

category.title: vector-handlers
category.p: Used for claiming RISC OS vectors (e.g., TickerV). For each entry, CMunge generates an assembly entry point and an extern void name(void) symbol in the C header. This symbol must never be called directly — pass its address to OS_Claim or similar. All such vector claims must be made in SVC mode, for example from initialisation code or a SWI handler.

The optional parameter error-capable: allows the handler to return an error: use the VECTOR_ERROR(err) macro instead of VECTOR_CLAIM. This sets the V flag and claims the vector, returning the error to the caller. All other registers are taken from the register block. If the err pointer is NULL, the result is equivalent to VECTOR_CLAIM.
C Prototype: int handler(_kernel_swi_regs *r, void *pw);
Return VECTOR_CLAIM or VECTOR_PASSON. The register block r->r[0] through r->r[9] contains the values on entry and can be updated to change the values returned to the caller.

category.title: irq-handlers
category.p: Obsolete alias for vector-handlers. New code should use vector-handlers instead.

category.title: vector-traps
category.p: (CMunge) Advanced vector chaining handler that provides access to the remainder of the vector chain. The register block passed in is the one with which the vector was called.
C Prototype: _kernel_oserror *handler(_kernel_swi_regs *r, void *pw, vectortrap_f trap, void *trappw);
The trap function can be called to invoke the remainder of the vector chain:

The register block passed to trap() is used to call the chain and is updated with the values returned. It may be the same block as the input or a different one. Any error returned by the chain can be inspected and re-returned or suppressed.
Return values from the handler:

definition-table.definition: Claim the vector without passing on.
Pass on to the remainder of the chain, as if return trap(r, trappw) had been called. This form reduces stack usage considerably and is preferred when rejecting uninteresting calls.
Claim the vector and return the error to the caller.

category.title: event-handler
category.p: Veneer for handling RISC OS events. Only one entry/handler pair may be given per event-handler directive, but the directive may be repeated to register multiple handlers. A list of event numbers may follow to enable fast accept/reject filtering — only those events will be passed to the handler. Providing event numbers is strongly recommended for performance.
C Prototype: int handler(_kernel_swi_regs *r, void *pw);
Return 0 to pass the event on, or non-zero to claim it.

category.title: error-chunk-base-number / error-base
category.p: The base error number for the module's error chunk. Error blocks are allocated in groups of 256. CMunge will accept any alignment so that sub-blocks can be re-used. The value is exported as ERROR_BASE in the C header.
The directive error-base is an accepted alias.

category.title: error-identifiers
category.p: Defines error constants exported to the C header as _kernel_oserror * via a macro to avoid copying to workspace.

category.title: library-initialisation-code
category.p: Sets the symbol for C library initialization, replacing the default _clib_initialisemodule. This is called before the C environment is fully set up, so the replacement routine must be written in assembly. It must call _clib_initialisemodule before returning.

category.title: library-enter-code
category.p: Sets the symbol for module entry, replacing the default _clib_entermodule.

category.title: international-help-file
category.p: Embeds a literal pathname in the module header for internationalized help messages. When the OS supports it, any help messages tagged as international in the command table will be looked up in this Messages file.

p.extended-example:
; CMunge description for a sample module title-string: SampleMod help-string: Sample 1.00 (06 Apr 2026)
initialisation-code: mod_init finalisation-code: mod_final
swi-chunk-base-number: &58B00 swi-handler-code: mod_swi swi-decoding-table: Sample, Get, Set
command-keyword-table: mod_command MyCmd(min-args: 0, max-args: 1, help-text: "Usage: *MyCmd [<arg>]")
generic-veneers: callback/callback_handler(private-word: r1)

disclaimer.p: Documentation based on CMunge manual and source code.

revision.author: GCLI
revision.title: Comprehensive documentation from Manual
revision.change: Updated with detailed information from Docs/Manual, including integer evaluation rules and specific handler prototypes.

revision.author: Gerph
revision.title: Additional directives, corrections and detail
revision.change: Added module-is-not-reentrant directive. Added irq-handlers alias. Added error-base alias and expanded error-chunk-base-number description. Improved library-initialisation-code and international-help-file descriptions. Added configure return codes. Added library-enter-code description. Added event-handler prototype and return value. Expanded vector-handlers with extern symbol usage and SVC mode requirement. Expanded generic-veneers with r12 preservation note. Expanded vector-traps with trap() usage and VECTORTRAP_PASSON behaviour. Expanded swi-decoding-code with full buffer protocol. Added command number parameter explanation and central handler '-' usage. Added service-call-handler cross-reference to -zbase.
